title: Production ML Operations
description: 🧠 Intuition: Production ML is where 90% of ML engineering happens — data pipelines, feature engineering, labeling, experiment design, deployment patterns, cost optimization. Most ML education focuses on model building, but the hard problems at FAANG are operational: how do you maintain data quality across 10K features? How do you label 1M examples cost-effectively? How do you run experiments when treatments interfere? This file covers 10 production ML topics with concrete frameworks, code, and FAANG-scale numbers.
details: 📋 Objectives: Master the operational skills that distinguish Staff+ ML engineers. Each section provides production-tested patterns with real code, concrete metrics, and FAANG interview angles.

sections.title: Online Learning & Bandits at Scale
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition Online learning updates the model incrementally as new data arrives, avoiding full retraining. Multi-armed bandits balance exploration (trying new options) vs exploitation (using what works). Thompson sampling treats each arm's reward as a Bayesian posterior — it naturally explores more when uncertain and exploits when confident. FAANG uses bandits for content recommendation, ad creative selection, and model A/B testing where traditional A/B tests waste traffic.

📊 Numbers Spotify's bandit for playlist recommendations: 2.3x improvement in engagement per session, 40% fewer skips. At 100M MAU, saving 1% of exploration traffic = $1M+/month in ad revenue. Thompson sampling with Beta(1,1) prior converges to optimal arm 2-4x faster than epsilon-greedy in sparse reward settings. Contextual bandits (arms depend on user features) outperform A/B tests by 35%+ when user heterogeneity is high.

🎯 Workflow 1) Define arms (model v1, v2, v3) and reward signal (CTR, watch time). 2) Initialize Beta(1,1) prior per arm. 3) For each request: sample from each Beta posterior, pick highest sample. 4) Observe reward (click = update Beta(alpha+1, beta), no click = Beta(alpha, beta+1)). 5) Monitor regret: difference between chosen arm and optimal arm over time. 6) Auto-promote winning arm after convergence (posterior overlap < 5%).

🔍 Interview Angle "Design a bandit system for ad creative selection." Walk through: arms are creatives, reward is CTR, Beta-Binomial conjugacy gives closed-form updates. Contextual bandit (LinUCB) if user features matter. Practical concern: delayed feedback — a click might come 10 minutes after impression; batch updates with staleness correction. FAANG scale: 10K+ arms, use Laplace approximation or bootstrapped Thompson sampling to avoid per-arm posterior updates.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from copy import deepcopy

class ThompsonSamplingBandit:
    """Beta-Binomial Thompson sampling for Bernoulli rewards.
    ponytail: conjugate prior is cheap but limited to binary rewards. For
    continuous rewards, use Normal-Inverse-Gamma; for 10K+ arms, use
    bootstrapped TS or Laplace approx (see chapel & lu 2021).
    """
    def __init__(self, n_arms: int, alpha_prior=1.0, beta_prior=1.0):
        self.alpha = np.full(n_arms, alpha_prior, dtype=float)
        self.beta  = np.full(n_arms, beta_prior,  dtype=float)
        self.n_arms = n_arms
        self.t = 0

    def select_arm(self) -> int:
        """Thompson sampling: sample from each posterior, pick max."""
        samples = np.random.beta(self.alpha, self.beta)
        return int(np.argmax(samples))

    def update(self, arm: int, reward: float):
        """Observe Bernoulli reward and update posterior."""
        self.t += 1
        self.alpha[arm] += reward
        self.beta[arm]  += 1.0 - reward

    def get_winning_arm(self) -> int:
        """Arm with highest expected reward after convergence."""
        return int(np.argmax(self.alpha / (self.alpha + self.beta)))

    # Contextual bandit: LinUCB with disjoint linear models per arm
    # x: d-dim feature vector, y: reward, lambda: ridge penalty
class LinUCB:
    def __init__(self, n_arms: int, n_features: int, alpha: float=0.2):
        self.n_arms = n_arms
        self.d = n_features
        self.alpha = alpha
        # A = X^T X + lambda*I, b = X^T y (per arm)
        self.A = [np.eye(n_features) for _ in range(n_arms)]
        self.b = [np.zeros(n_features) for _ in range(n_arms)]

    def select_arm(self, x: np.ndarray) -> int:
        """Pick arm with highest UCB: theta^T x + alpha * sqrt(x^T A^-1 x)"""
        best_arm, best_ucb = 0, -np.inf
        for arm in range(self.n_arms):
            theta = np.linalg.solve(self.A[arm], self.b[arm])
            mean = theta @ x
            uncertainty = self.alpha * np.sqrt(x @ np.linalg.solve(self.A[arm], x))
            ucb = mean + uncertainty
            if ucb > best_ucb:
                best_ucb, best_arm = ucb, arm
        return best_arm

    def update(self, arm: int, x: np.ndarray, reward: float):
        self.A[arm] += np.outer(x, x)
        self.b[arm] += reward * x

sections.title: Feature Engineering Platform
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition A feature store (Feast, Tecton) centralizes feature management: write once, use in training and serving. The key challenge is point-in-time correctness — when training, each label must be joined with feature values that were actually available at prediction time. Using future data (data leakage) causes optimistic eval that fails in production. Online features (low-latency, current values for serving) and offline features (historical snapshots for training) must be consistent. Feature validation (range, null rate, distribution) runs before materialization.

📊 Numbers Uber's feature platform (Michelangelo): 10K+ features, 100M+ daily predictions. Without a feature store, teams duplicate 40-60% of feature engineering work. Point-in-time correctness catches 15-20% of training-serving skew issues at verification. Feast reduces feature deployment time from weeks to days. Online feature latency target: <10ms p99 at 100K QPS via Redis or DynamoDB-backed serving.

🎯 Workflow 1) Define feature views with source, entities, TTL. 2) Register features in the feature registry via Python SDK. 3) Run batch materialization to offline store (S3/Parquet) and online store (Redis/DynamoDB). 4) Training data retrieval: historical retrieval with point-in-time join. 5) Serving: online retrieval by entity key from online store. 6) Monitor: feature freshness lag, null rates, range violations.

🔍 Interview Angle "Design a feature platform for a 1000-model org." Walk through: Feast-style architecture with offline (Spark on Parquet in S3) and online (Redis cluster) stores. Point-in-time join via timestamp-based ASOF joins. Feature serving SLA: <10ms, 99.9th percentile. Feature governance: owner, versioning, validation suite per feature. Feature discovery via registry search.
sections.codeBlock:
from feast import FeatureStore, Entity, FeatureView, Field, FeatureService
from feast.infra.offline_stores.file_source import FileSource
from feast.types import Float32, Int64, String
from datetime import timedelta

# Define entity
user_entity = Entity(
    name="user_id",
    value_type=Int64,
    description="Unique user identifier",
    join_keys=["user_id"],
)

# Define feature source (parquet in S3 or local)
user_stats_source = FileSource(
    path="s3://feature-bucket/user_stats/*.parquet",
    timestamp_field="event_timestamp",
    created_timestamp_column="created_timestamp",
)

# Define feature view with point-in-time semantics
user_stats_features = FeatureView(
    name="user_stats",
    entities=[user_entity],
    ttl=timedelta(days=7),  # Features expire after 7 days stale
    schema=[
        Field(name="avg_purchase_7d", dtype=Float32),
        Field(name="num_sessions_7d", dtype=Int64),
        Field(name="total_mins_7d", dtype=Float32),
        Field(name="churn_score_30d", dtype=Float32),
    ],
    online=True,  # Materialize to online store for serving
    source=user_stats_source,
)

# Feature service for grouped retrieval
user_modeling_features = FeatureService(
    name="user_modeling_v1",
    features=[user_stats_features],
)

# Usage in training: point-in-time correct historical retrieval
store = FeatureStore(repo_path="./feature_repo")
# training_df = store.get_historical_features(
#     entity_df=entity_df,  # DataFrame with user_id, event_timestamp, label
#     features=user_modeling_features,
# ).to_df()
#
# Usage in serving: real-time feature retrieval
# features = store.get_online_features(
#     features=user_modeling_features,
#     entity_rows=[{"user_id": 12345}],
# ).to_dict()

# Validation: register expectations per feature
# from great_expectations.dataset import PandasDataset
# ds = PandasDataset(training_df)
# ds.expect_column_values_to_be_between("avg_purchase_7d", 0, 100000)

sections.title: Data Quality Monitoring Pipelines
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition Data quality in ML is multi-layered: schema validation (expected columns, types), range validation (feature values within expected bounds), distributional validation (feature distributions haven't shifted), and integrity validation (null rates, uniqueness, referential integrity). At FAANG scale, data quality checks run at multiple points: ingest (raw data arrives), transform (after feature engineering), and pre-training (training dataset). Bad data that reaches production models causes silent degradation that can persist for weeks.

📊 Numbers Google's TFX data validation catches 30% of production issues before they affect models. Uber: 40% of ML incidents are traced to data quality issues. Great Expectations with 50-100 expectations per pipeline catches 85%+ of data anomalies. Feature-level alerts: PSI > 0.25 triggers investigation. A single corrupted feature pipeline can degrade model AUC by 0.05-0.10 across 25% of predictions.

🎯 Workflow 1) Define expectations per dataset: column presence, types, min, max, unique values, distribution quantiles. 2) Run validations on data batches daily/hourly. 3) Publish results to data quality dashboard (grafana). 4) Alert on critical failures: schema mismatch, null rate > 5%, distribution shift > 3 sigma. 5) Block training pipeline if validation fails. 6) Quarterly expectation review and update.

🔍 Interview Angle "How do you detect a corrupted feature pipeline in production?" Walk through: monitoring feature distributions (PSI, KS test) vs baseline. Sudden spike in null rate. Schema drift (new column, deleted column). Check prediction distribution — if feature X went missing, predictions cluster at a single value. Root cause: upstream pipeline change, corrupted source data, bug in feature transform.
sections.codeBlock:
import great_expectations as ge
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Define a Great Expectations suite for ML feature data
# Run after feature engineering, before training
def create_ml_data_suite():
    """Return an ExpectationSuite for ML training data validation."""
    suite = ge.core.ExpectationSuite(
        expectation_suite_name="ml_training_data_v3"
    )

    # Schema expectations
    suite.add_expectation(
        ge.core.ExpectationConfiguration(
            expectation_type="expect_table_columns_to_match_set",
            kwargs={"column_set": [
                "user_id", "item_id", "feature_1", "feature_2",
                "feature_3", "feature_4", "label"
            ]},
        )
    )

    # Null rate expectations (< 5% nulls per feature)
    for col in ["feature_1", "feature_2", "feature_3", "feature_4"]:
        suite.add_expectation(
            ge.core.ExpectationConfiguration(
                expectation_type="expect_column_values_to_not_be_null",
                kwargs={"column": col, "mostly": 0.95},
            )
        )

    # Range expectations per feature
    suite.add_expectation(
        ge.core.ExpectationConfiguration(
            expectation_type="expect_column_values_to_be_between",
            kwargs={
                "column": "feature_1",
                "min_value": 0.0,
                "max_value": 1.0,
            },
        )
    )
    suite.add_expectation(
        ge.core.ExpectationConfiguration(
            expectation_type="expect_column_values_to_be_between",
            kwargs={
                "column": "label",
                "min_value": 0,
                "max_value": 1,
            },
        )
    )

    # Distributional expectation: mean within expected range
    suite.add_expectation(
        ge.core.ExpectationConfiguration(
            expectation_type="expect_column_mean_to_be_between",
            kwargs={
                "column": "feature_2",
                "min_value": 0.2,
                "max_value": 0.6,
            },
        )
    )

    return suite

# Usage in pipeline:
# df = pd.read_parquet("training_data.parquet")
# ge_df = ge.from_pandas(df)
# suite = create_ml_data_suite()
# results = ge_df.validate(suite, result_format="COMPLETE")
# if not results["success"]:
#     raise ValueError(f"Data validation failed: {results['statistics']}")
#
# For streaming: use Deequ (Spark) or whylogs at ingest
# Feature-level drift: PSI per feature, auto-alert on threshold

def compute_psi(expected: np.array, actual: np.array, bins=10) -> float:
    """Population Stability Index — <0.1 stable, 0.1-0.25 monitor, >0.25 alert."""
    expected_perc = np.histogram(expected, bins=bins, range=(0, 1))[0] / len(expected)
    actual_perc   = np.histogram(actual,   bins=bins, range=(0, 1))[0] / len(actual)
    expected_perc = np.clip(expected_perc, 1e-4, 1.0)
    actual_perc   = np.clip(actual_perc,   1e-4, 1.0)
    return float(np.sum((actual_perc - expected_perc) * np.log(actual_perc / expected_perc)))

sections.title: Labeling Strategy at Scale (Active Learning, Weak Supervision, Snorkel)
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition Labeling is the most expensive part of supervised ML at scale. Three approaches reduce cost: active learning selects the most informative examples for human labeling (uncertainty sampling, diversity sampling, expected model change), weak supervision uses heuristic labeling functions (regex patterns, knowledge bases, external APIs) and combines their noisy outputs into probabilistic labels, Snorkel models the accuracies of labeling functions using a generative model, reweighting them without ground truth. FAANG combines all three: weak supervision for coverage, active learning for hard cases, and Snorkel's label model to denoise.

📊 Numbers Snorkel at Google: labeling cost reduced by 70-90% compared to full manual labeling while maintaining 90%+ of supervised accuracy. Active learning reduces label requirements by 50-80% compared to random sampling (depending on task difficulty). Weak supervision with 20-50 labeling functions typically reaches 80-85% of human label quality. A typical FAANG team spends $500K-$2M/year on labeling; active+weak reduces this to $100K-$400K.

🎯 Workflow 1) Write heuristic labeling functions (domain rules, keyword matches, external model outputs). 2) Train Snorkel generative label model to estimate LF accuracies and correlations. 3) Generate probabilistic training labels for all unlabeled data. 4) Train downstream model on probabilistic labels (or use a noise-aware loss). 5) Run active learning on the trained model's uncertainty scores to select 5-10% of examples for human review. 6) Iterate: add/improve LFs, retrain label model, fine-tune downstream model.

🔍 Interview Angle "You have 1M unlabeled examples and a labeling budget of 10K. How do you maximize model performance?" Walk through: combine weak supervision (10-20 LFs, Snorkel label model to get 500K labeled) + active learning (pick 10K hardest from the 500K where the label model is uncertain) + train final model on 510K examples. Cost: $0 vs $50K for full manual labeling. Fallback: if LFs are poor (accuracy < 60%), invest more in human labels for the active learning set.
sections.codeBlock:
# Snorkel: weak supervision with generative label model
# pip install snorkel

from snorkel.labeling import labeling_function, PandasLFApplier
from snorkel.labeling.model import LabelModel
from snorkel.labeling import LFAnalysis
import pandas as pd

# Define abstraction for label values
ABSTAIN = -1
POSITIVE = 1
NEGATIVE = 0

# Heuristic labeling functions
@labeling_function()
def keyword_spam(x):
    """LF 1: keyword match for spam detection."""
    spam_keywords = ["buy now", "act now", "limited time", "click here"]
    return POSITIVE if any(kw in x["text"].lower() for kw in spam_keywords) else ABSTAIN

@labeling_function()
def url_shortener(x):
    """LF 2: shortened URLs."""
    import re
    return POSITIVE if re.search(r"bit\.ly|tinyurl\.com|t\.co", x["text"].lower()) else ABSTAIN

@labeling_function()
def all_caps_title(x):
    """LF 3: all-caps title."""
    words = x["title"].split()
    if len(words) > 3 and sum(1 for w in words if w.isupper()) / len(words) > 0.8:
        return POSITIVE
    return ABSTAIN

@labeling_function()
def benign_sender(x):
    """LF 4: known benign sender."""
    benign = {"newsletter@", "noreply@", "support@"}
    return NEGATIVE if any(x["sender"].startswith(b) for b in benign) else ABSTAIN

@labeling_function()
def high_information_score(x):
    """LF 5: negative if from verified source."""
    return NEGATIVE if x.get("is_verified", False) else ABSTAIN

# Apply all LFs to unlabeled data
lfs = [keyword_spam, url_shortener, all_caps_title, benign_sender, high_information_score]
applier = PandasLFApplier(lfs)
L_train = applier.apply(df_unlabeled)

# Analyze labeling functions
lf_stats = LFAnalysis(L_train, lfs).lf_summary()
print(lf_stats)
# Coverage should be >60%, accuracy vs a small labeled set should be >70%

# Train generative label model (no ground truth needed!)
label_model = LabelModel(cardinality=2, verbose=True)
label_model.fit(L_train, n_epochs=500, log_freq=100, seed=123)

# Get probabilistic labels
prob_labels = label_model.predict_proba(L_train)[:, POSITIVE]
# Use these as soft labels, or threshold to get hard labels
# hard_labels = (prob_labels > 0.5).astype(int)

# Train downstream model on probabilistic labels
# from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
# clf = LogisticRegression()
# clf.fit(X_features, prob_labels)  # Soft labels

# Active learning: uncertainty sampling on downstream model probs
# from modAL import uncertainty_sampling
# query_idx, _ = uncertainty_sampling(clf, X_pool, n_instances=1000)

sections.title: Experimentation with Network Effects
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition Standard A/B tests assume no interference between treatment and control groups. In social networks, two-sided markets, and recommendation systems, this assumption fails: showing a better feed to treatment users changes how they interact with control users (they share, comment, tag). This interference biases the treatment effect estimate. Solutions: switchback experiments (alternate time windows), cluster-randomized trials (randomize by network cluster), and difference-in-differences (compare before/after slopes). Meta/LinkedIn/Uber all use these for network-effect-sensitive metrics.

📊 Numbers LinkedIn found that naive A/B tests overestimate treatment effects by 30-70% on feed ranking changes due to network interference. Uber's switchback experiments for pricing: 95% power with 3x longer duration vs standard A/B, but unbiased. Switchback experiments need 10-20 switches minimum for statistical validity. Cluster-randomized trials with 50+ clusters achieve 80% power at 10% MDE. Difference-in-differences reduces bias by 40-60% when parallel trends hold.

🎯 Workflow 1) Assess interference risk: does treatment affect the same metric for control users via social/economic links? 2) If interference is suspected, choose design: switchback for time-varying interference (pricing, marketplace), cluster-randomized for social network interference (feed, recommendations). 3) Run experiment for minimum 3-5 switch periods (switchback) or with 50+ clusters. 4) Analyze using cluster-robust standard errors. 5) Validate with design-based sensitivity analysis: vary interference assumptions and check estimate stability.

🔍 Interview Angle "Your A/B test shows +5% engagement but after rollout the effect is -2%. Why?" — Network interference: treatment users engaged more and pulled content from control users' feeds, making control worse. Solution: switchback experiment at the time level (randomize 1-hour windows) to isolate system-level effects. At Uber: pricing experiments use switchback to avoid driver/rider interference. At Meta: feed experiments use cluster-randomization by graph partition.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from scipy import stats

# Switchback experiment design for a marketplace platform
# Randomize time windows, not users, to avoid network interference

np.random.seed(42)
n_switches = 20       # Minimum 10-20 switches for validity
hours_per_switch = 2  # 2-hour blocks
total_hours = n_switches * hours_per_switch

# Generate switch assignment
switch_assignments = np.random.choice(["treatment", "control"], size=n_switches, p=[0.5, 0.5])

# Simulate hourly metric (e.g., conversion rate) under treatment and control
# Treatment lifts conversion by 3% but also suppresses control via interference
hours = np.arange(total_hours)
base_conversion = 0.05  # 5% baseline

conversion = np.zeros(total_hours)
for i in range(n_switches):
    start = i * hours_per_switch
    end = start + hours_per_switch
    is_treatment = switch_assignments[i] == "treatment"
    # Treatment gets +3%, control gets -1% due to interference
    effect = 0.03 if is_treatment else -0.01
    conversion[start:end] = base_conversion + effect + np.random.normal(0, 0.005, hours_per_switch)

# Analyze with cluster-robust standard errors (by switch block)
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "hour": hours,
    "conversion": conversion,
    "switch_block": np.repeat(np.arange(n_switches), hours_per_switch),
    "treatment": np.repeat(switch_assignments == "treatment", hours_per_switch).astype(int),
})

# Switchblock-level means (cluster-level analysis)
block_means = df.groupby(["switch_block", "treatment"])["conversion"].mean().reset_index()
treat_group = block_means[block_means["treatment"] == 1]["conversion"]
ctrl_group  = block_means[block_means["treatment"] == 0]["conversion"]

# Welch's t-test on cluster means
t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(treat_group, ctrl_group, equal_var=False)
effect = treat_group.mean() - ctrl_group.mean()

print(f"Switchback ATE: {effect:.4f} ({effect/base_conversion*100:+.1f}%)")
print(f"p-value: {p_value:.4f}")
print(f"Naive (user-level) would have been biased by {0.30:.0%}+ if interference exists")

# Difference-in-Differences for before/after analysis
# y = beta_0 + beta_1 * treat + beta_2 * post + beta_3 * treat*post
# beta_3 is the DiD estimator

# Cluster-randomized design: randomize by network cluster
# Analyze with cluster-robust HC2 standard errors:
# from linearmodels.panel import compare, PanelOLS
# mod = PanelOLS.from_formula(
#     "y ~ treatment + ClusterEffects",
#     data=df, cluster_entity="cluster_id"
# )

sections.title: Model Cascades & Ensembles in Production
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition A model cascade routes easy examples through a cheap model and only escalates hard examples to an expensive model. This is the most cost-effective deployment pattern at scale: 80-90% of inferences go through a small model (logistic regression, small XGBoost) at <1ms, while only 10-20% go through a deep neural net at 50-100ms. Ensembles combine multiple models for accuracy but multiply cost. The practical trade: cascades optimize cost, ensembles optimize accuracy; cascades + final ensemble head combine both.

📊 Numbers Google's cascade in production ad systems: logistic regression model handles 85% of traffic at 0.5ms, deep model handles 15% at 50ms — 5x latency reduction at 0.999% of full-model accuracy. Netflix's ensemble of 10+ recommendation models: 8-12% lift in engagement vs single model. Real-world: cascade can reduce total inference cost by 60-80% while sacrificing <0.5% accuracy. Bagging reduces variance by 1/sqrt(M) where M is ensemble size; stacking adds 1-5% over best single model.

🎯 Workflow 1) Train a cheap model (high recall, moderate precision). 2) Train an expensive model (high precision). 3) Define cascade threshold: if cheap model's confidence > 0.9 OR < 0.1, return immediately; otherwise escalate. 4) Monitor: cascade rate (% escalated), latency distribution, accuracy per tier. 5) For ensembles: train diverse models (different architectures, feature sets, seeds), combine with weighted average or learned stacker. 6) A/B test cascade vs single-model to validate cost savings without accuracy loss.

🔍 Interview Angle "Design a fraud detection system that handles 10K QPS with <20ms p99 latency and 99% accuracy." — Cascade: shallow XGBoost (1ms, 95% recall) handles 90% of traffic. Cases with confidence < 0.8 pass to GNN (50ms, 99.5% precision). Only 10% escalate, effective latency = 0.9*1ms + 0.1*50ms = 5.9ms. For accuracy: if both models disagree, fall through to a gradient-boosted decision tree on escalated features.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from typing import Tuple, Any

class ModelCascade:
    """Route easy examples through cheap model, escalate hard ones to expensive.
    ponytail: thresholds are static here; in production, tune threshold
    on a held-out set to minimize cost at target accuracy. For dynamic
    cost-aware cascades, use a learned router (reinforcement learning).
    """
    def __init__(
        self,
        cheap_model,
        expensive_model,
        low_conf_thresh: float = 0.15,
        high_conf_thresh: float = 0.85,
    ):
        self.cheap = cheap_model
        self.expensive = expensive_model
        self.low = low_conf_thresh   # Below this → predict negative
        self.high = high_conf_thresh  # Above this → predict positive
        self.cheap_count = 0
        self.expensive_count = 0

    def predict(self, features: np.ndarray) -> Tuple[int, float, str]:
        """Returns (prediction, confidence, tier_used)."""
        proba = self.cheap.predict_proba(features)[:, 1]  # shape (n,)
        self.cheap_count += 1

        # Cheap model is confident → return immediately
        confident_negative = proba < self.low
        confident_positive = proba > self.high
        confident = confident_negative | confident_positive

        predictions = np.full(len(features), -1, dtype=int)
        tiers = np.full(len(features), "cheap", dtype=object)

        predictions[confident_negative] = 0
        predictions[confident_positive] = 1
        tiers[confident] = "cheap"

        # Escalate uncertain examples to expensive model
        uncertain = ~confident
        if uncertain.any():
            self.expensive_count += uncertain.sum()
            proba_exp = self.expensive.predict_proba(features[uncertain])[:, 1]
            predictions[uncertain] = (proba_exp > 0.5).astype(int)
            tiers[uncertain] = "expensive"
            proba[uncertain] = proba_exp

        return predictions, proba, tiers

    @property
    def cascade_rate(self) -> float:
        total = self.cheap_count + self.expensive_count
        if total == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.expensive_count / total


# Ensemble: weighted average of multiple models
class WeightedEnsemble:
    """Weighted average ensemble with optional learned weights."""
    def __init__(self, models: list, weights: np.ndarray = None):
        self.models = models
        self.n_models = len(models)
        self.weights = weights if weights is not None else np.ones(self.n_models) / self.n_models

    def predict_proba(self, X: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        # Get predictions from all models
        preds = np.array([m.predict_proba(X)[:, 1] for m in self.models])
        # Weighted average
        return np.dot(self.weights, preds)

    def predict(self, X: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        return (self.predict_proba(X) > 0.5).astype(int)

    # Stacking: train a meta-model on base model outputs
    # from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
    # meta = LogisticRegression()
    # base_preds = np.column_stack([m.predict_proba(X)[:, 1] for m in models])
    # meta.fit(base_preds, y)

sections.title: Continuous Training & Deployment Patterns
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition Models in production must be continuously retrained to avoid performance degradation from data drift. Trigger-based retraining (drift detected, data accumulated beyond threshold) is more efficient than schedule-based (hourly/daily retrain regardless). Shadow deployment runs the new model alongside the old one without serving its predictions — compare outputs, no user risk. Canary sends 1-5% of traffic to the new model. Blue-green switches entire traffic at once between two full environments. Gradual rollout increases traffic in steps (5% → 20% → 50% → 100%) with automated rollback at each stage if metrics regress.

📊 Numbers Uber retrains 10K+ models daily: 60% scheduled, 30% drift-triggered, 10% manual. Canary at 1% detects 90% of critical regressions with <0.1% user impact. Shadow deployment catches 80% of training-serving skew issues before any user sees them. Blue-green cuts deployment time from 30min to 2min but has full blast radius. Gradual rollout with 5 stages reduces blast radius by 20x vs blue-green — the standard for user-facing models.

🎯 Workflow 1) Choose retraining trigger: schedule (every 6h) + drift (if PSI > 0.2) + data volume (if 100K new samples). 2) Validate new model against holdout: performance must be within 1% of champion or show significant improvement. 3) Shadow deploy for 24h: compare prediction distributions, latency, error rates. 4) Canary 1% (6h) → 10% (12h) → 50% (12h) → 100%. 5) Each stage: monitor business metrics, latency, error rate, prediction distribution. 6) Auto-rollback if any metric exceeds 2x baseline for 5 consecutive minutes.

🔍 Interview Angle "How do you deploy a model update to 500M users safely?" — Gradual rollout with automated canary analysis: 0.1% → 1% → 10% → 50% → 100%. At each stage, a statistical test compares the treatment metric against a holdout baseline. If p < 0.05 with negative direction or metric drops > 1%, auto-rollback in 60 seconds. Shadow deployment for 48h first to catch any immediate issues. Feature flags for instant kill switch independent of code deployment.
sections.codeBlock:
# Kubernetes deployment config for ML model rollout with canary
# Save as canary-deployment.yaml and apply with:
# kubectl apply -f canary-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ml-model-champion  # Current production model
spec:
  replicas: 10
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ml-model
      version: champion
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ml-model
        version: champion
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: model-server
        image: registry/ml-model:v1.2.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        env:
        - name: MODEL_VERSION
          value: "champion"
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "4Gi"
            cpu: "2"
          limits:
            memory: "8Gi"
            cpu: "4"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 15
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ml-model-challenger  # Candidate model under canary
spec:
  replicas: 1  # 10% canary (1 pod vs 10 champion pods)
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ml-model
      version: challenger
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ml-model
        version: challenger
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: model-server
        image: registry/ml-model:v1.3.0-rc1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        env:
        - name: MODEL_VERSION
          value: "challenger"
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "4Gi"
            cpu: "2"
          limits:
            memory: "8Gi"
            cpu: "4"
---
# Service routes traffic based on weight
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: ml-model-service
spec:
  selector:
    app: ml-model
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
# Use Istio or Flagger for traffic splitting:
# apiVersion: flagger.app/v1beta1
# kind: Canary
# spec:
#   targetRef:
#     apiVersion: apps/v1
#     kind: Deployment
#     name: ml-model
#   service:
#     port: 80
#   analysis:
#     interval: 5m
#     threshold: 5
#     maxWeight: 50
#     stepWeight: 5
#     metrics:
#     - name: request-success-rate
#       threshold: 99
#     - name: request-duration
#       threshold: 500
#     webhooks:
#     - name: model-prediction-drift
#       url: http://monitor/predictions/drift
#       timeout: 5s
# Automated retraining trigger
import datetime

def should_retrain(last_train: datetime.datetime, samples_since_train: int,
                   drift_psi: float, max_stale_hours: int = 24,
                   min_samples: int = 100_000, drift_threshold: float = 0.25) -> str:
    hours_stale = (datetime.datetime.utcnow() - last_train).total_seconds() / 3600
    if hours_stale > max_stale_hours:
        return "schedule"
    if drift_psi > drift_threshold:
        return "drift"
    if samples_since_train > min_samples:
        return "data_volume"
    return "none"

sections.title: Budget-Aware Model Selection
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition Model selection in production must account for inference cost as a first-class metric alongside accuracy. A model that's 0.5% more accurate but 10x more expensive might be a bad choice at scale. The key metric is accuracy per dollar — or more precisely, the Pareto frontier of accuracy vs latency/compute. Model compression (quantization, pruning, distillation) directly improves this ratio. At FAANG scale, a 1ms per-request savings at 1B requests/day saves 1M ms = ~277 compute-hours daily = $500K+/year in cloud costs.

📊 Numbers Google's universal sentence encoder: 50x smaller than BERT-Base, retains 90%+ of downstream performance. Quantization (float32 → int8) reduces model size 4x, latency 2-3x, accuracy loss 0.1-0.5%. Distillation (teacher-student): 98% of teacher accuracy at 10% of the cost. Pareto analysis across model architectures: typically 10-20 models evaluated, 3-5 on the Pareto frontier. Optimum latency: <10ms for real-time, <100ms for near-real-time, batch for offline.

🎯 Workflow 1) Define cost function: model FLOPS, memory (RAM/VRAM), latency p99, throughput (req/s/$). 2) Train or collect 10-20 candidate models across architectures. 3) Profile each: latency at batch sizes [1, 8, 32, 64] on target hardware, memory usage. 4) Compute accuracy-per-cost ratio. 5) Plot Pareto frontier: accuracy (y) vs cost (x). 6) Pick the model at the knee of the curve. 7) Apply compression: int8 quantization, distil if compute budget is tight. 8) Validate compressed model accuracy, deploy.

🔍 Interview Angle "You need to serve a BERT-based model at 10K QPS with <20ms latency on a $5K/month budget. What do you do?" — Option 1: DistilBERT (40ms, 97% accuracy, fits on 1 GPU). Option 2: TinyBERT + int8 quantization (10ms, 95% accuracy, 2 CPU cores). Option 3: cascade — small logistic regression (1ms) for 80%, DistilBERT for 20%. Option 3 wins: effective latency = 0.8*1 + 0.2*40 = 8.8ms, total cost $2K/month, accuracy 96.6%. Present the Pareto frontier with at least 3 candidate architectures.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from typing import List, Dict

class CostAwareModelSelector:
    """Pareto-optimal model selection considering accuracy AND cost.
    ponytail: uses static cost measurements; in production, costs vary
    with traffic patterns, hardware provisioning, and spot instance pricing.
    For dynamic cost-accuracy optimization, use a learned cost model.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.models = []  # List of dicts: name, accuracy, latency_ms, cost_per_1k

    def add_model(self, name: str, accuracy: float, latency_p99_ms: float,
                  cost_per_1k_inferences: float):
        self.models.append({
            "name": name,
            "accuracy": accuracy,
            "latency_ms": latency_p99_ms,
            "cost_per_1k": cost_per_1k_inferences,
            "acc_per_latency": accuracy / latency_p99_ms * 1000,
            "acc_per_dollar": accuracy / cost_per_1k_inferences * 100,
        })

    def pareto_frontier(self, cost_metric: str = "cost_per_1k") -> pd.DataFrame:
        """Return models on the Pareto frontier (no other model is both
        more accurate AND cheaper on the given cost metric)."""
        df = pd.DataFrame(self.models)
        df = df.sort_values("accuracy", ascending=False)
        pareto = []
        min_cost = float("inf")
        for _, row in df.iterrows():
            if row[cost_metric] < min_cost:
                pareto.append(row)
                min_cost = row[cost_metric]
        return pd.DataFrame(pareto)

    def recommend(self, max_latency: float = None, max_cost: float = None,
                  min_accuracy: float = None) -> Dict:
        """Recommend the best model given constraints."""
        candidates = pd.DataFrame(self.models)
        if max_latency:
            candidates = candidates[candidates["latency_ms"] <= max_latency]
        if max_cost:
            candidates = candidates[candidates["cost_per_1k"] <= max_cost]
        if min_accuracy:
            candidates = candidates[candidates["accuracy"] >= min_accuracy]
        if candidates.empty:
            return {"error": "No model satisfies constraints. Relax constraints or use cascade."}
        # Pick the model with highest accuracy-per-cost
        best = candidates.loc[candidates["acc_per_dollar"].idxmax()]
        return best.to_dict()


# Example: model comparison
selector = CostAwareModelSelector()
selector.add_model("LogisticRegression", accuracy=0.82, latency_p99_ms=0.5, cost_per_1k=0.001)
selector.add_model("XGBoost",            accuracy=0.91, latency_p99_ms=2.0, cost_per_1k=0.005)
selector.add_model("LightGBM",           accuracy=0.90, latency_p99_ms=1.5, cost_per_1k=0.004)
selector.add_model("DistilBERT",         accuracy=0.95, latency_p99_ms=15.0, cost_per_1k=0.05)
selector.add_model("BERT-Base",          accuracy=0.97, latency_p99_ms=50.0, cost_per_1k=0.15)
selector.add_model("TinyBERT+int8",      accuracy=0.94, latency_p99_ms=8.0, cost_per_1k=0.02)

print(selector.recommend(max_latency=20, max_cost=0.10, min_accuracy=0.90))
print("Pareto frontier:")
print(selector.pareto_frontier())

# Model compression: quantization-aware training
import tensorflow as tf

# converter = tf.lite.TFLiteConverter.from_keras_model(model)
# converter.optimizations = [tf.lite.Optimize.DEFAULT]
# converter.representative_dataset = representative_dataset
# quantized_model = converter.convert()  # float32 -> int8, 4x smaller, 2-3x faster

sections.title: Model Performance Degradation & Root Cause Analysis
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition When a production model degrades (accuracy drops, predictions shift, latency spikes), the root cause falls into one of four categories: data failures (missing features, corrupted source, stale pipeline), manifestation failures (drift in feature distributions, new categories that weren't in training), computation failures (hardware issue, OOM, latency spike), or feedback loop failures (model's own predictions change future data distribution). Isolating which category is the first step. FAANG teams run a standard incident triage playbook that starts with checking data pipeline health.

📊 Numbers Google: 60% of ML production incidents are data-related (missing features, stale pipeline, schema change). 25% are drift-related (data or concept). 10% are infrastructure-related. 5% are labeling pipeline issues (bad ground truth causing wrong evaluation). Average incident detection time: 15-30 minutes in mature organizations vs 2-4 hours in ad-hoc setups. Mean time to root cause: 1-4 hours. Cost of undetected degradation: 1-5% revenue impact per week.

🎯 Workflow 1) Detect: prediction distribution shift, accuracy monitoring (if labels available), latency/error alerts. 2) Triage: check data pipeline health (freshness, null rates), then feature drift (PSI per feature), then model serving (error rates, memory). 3) Isolate: compare failed vs healthy period. Slice by feature values, geography, time of day. 4) Root cause: fix data bug / retrain with recent data / rollback model version. 5) Remediate: fix, roll forward, add monitoring to prevent recurrence. 6) Postmortem: document timeline, root cause, action items.

🔍 Interview Angle "Your model's AUC dropped from 0.92 to 0.81 overnight. Walk through the debugging process." — Step 1: Check if it's an evaluation issue (label pipeline broken, wrong ground truth). If labels are correct, Step 2: Check prediction distribution — did all predictions shift? If yes, is it data drift (check PSI on features) or concept drift (ground truth generation changed)? Step 3: Check feature pipeline — any nulls, any schema changes? Step 4: Check if a new feature was added that leaked the label. Most common root cause at FAANG: a data pipeline change that went unnoticed.
sections.codeBlock:
# Root cause analysis template for ML production incidents
# Run this as a notebook to investigate a suspected degradation

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

INCIDENT_TIME = datetime(2026, 6, 23, 14, 30)  # When alert fired
LOOKBACK_HOURS = 24

class MLIncidentRCA:
    """Systematic root cause analysis template for model degradation.
    ponytail: assumes recent data is accessible; in prod, query
    monitoring system (Prometheus, WhyLabs, Arize) via API.
    """
    def __init__(self, model_name: str):
        self.model = model_name
        self.findings = []

    def check_data_pipeline(self) -> bool:
        """Step 1: Check data source freshness and null rates."""
        print("=== STEP 1: Data Pipeline Health ===")
        # In production: query feature store monitoring
        # fresh = monitoring.get_feature_freshness(model=self.model)
        # null_rates = monitoring.get_null_rates(model=self.model, hours=LOOKBACK_HOURS)
        fresh = datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(minutes=15)  # Mock: data is fresh
        cutoff = datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(hours=1)
        if fresh < cutoff:
            self.findings.append("DATA_STALE: Feature pipeline hasn't updated in >1h")
            return False
        # null_rates = {"feature_1": 0.02, "feature_2": 0.50, "feature_3": 0.01}
        # high_nulls = [f for f, r in null_rates.items() if r > 0.05]
        high_nulls = ["feature_2"]  # Mock
        if high_nulls:
            self.findings.append(f"NULL_SPIKE: Features {high_nulls} have null rate > 5%")
            return False
        print("Data pipeline: OK")
        return True

    def check_feature_drift(self, recent_features: pd.DataFrame,
                            baseline_features: pd.DataFrame) -> bool:
        """Step 2: Check PSI per feature, flag >0.25."""
        print("=== STEP 2: Feature Drift Analysis ===")
        drifted = []
        for col in recent_features.columns:
            psi = self._compute_psi(baseline_features[col], recent_features[col])
            if psi > 0.25:
                drifted.append((col, psi))
        if drifted:
            for col, psi in drifted:
                self.findings.append(f"DRIFT: {col} PSI={psi:.3f} (>0.25)")
            return False
        print("Feature drift: None detected")
        return True

    def check_prediction_shift(self, recent_preds: np.ndarray,
                                baseline_preds: np.ndarray) -> bool:
        """Step 3: Check if prediction distribution shifted."""
        print("=== STEP 3: Prediction Distribution Shift ===")
        from scipy.stats import ks_2samp
        stat, pval = ks_2samp(baseline_preds, recent_preds)
        if pval < 0.01:
            self.findings.append(f"PRED_SHIFT: KS stat={stat:.3f}, p={pval:.4f}")
            return False
        print(f"Prediction distribution: stable (KS p={pval:.4f})")
        return True

    def check_label_leakage(self, features: pd.DataFrame, labels: np.ndarray) -> bool:
        """Step 4: Check if any feature perfectly correlates with label (leakage)."""
        print("=== STEP 4: Label Leakage Check ===")
        for col in features.columns:
            corr = np.abs(np.corrcoef(features[col].fillna(0), labels)[0, 1])
            if corr > 0.99:
                self.findings.append(f"LEAKAGE: {col} correlates r={corr:.4f} with label")
                return False
        print("Label leakage: None detected")
        return True

    def generate_report(self) -> str:
        print("\n=== RCA REPORT ===")
        if not self.findings:
            return "No findings. Check infrastructure layer (GPU, memory, networking)."
        return "\n".join(f"- {f}" for f in self.findings)

    @staticmethod
    def _compute_psi(expected, actual, bins=10) -> float:
        eps = 1e-4
        exp_perc = np.histogram(expected, bins=bins, range=(0, 1))[0] / len(expected)
        act_perc = np.histogram(actual, bins=bins, range=(0, 1))[0] / len(actual)
        exp_perc = np.clip(exp_perc, eps, 1.0)
        act_perc = np.clip(act_perc, eps, 1.0)
        return float(np.sum((act_perc - exp_perc) * np.log(act_perc / exp_perc)))


# Example usage:
# rca = MLIncidentRCA("fraud-detection-v3")
# rca.check_data_pipeline()
# rca.check_feature_drift(recent_feats, baseline_feats)
# rca.check_prediction_shift(recent_preds, baseline_preds)
# rca.check_label_leakage(features, labels)
# print(rca.generate_report())

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — Production ML
sections.description:
🔍 Interview Angle Staff+ ML interviews test operational depth. Below are 5 high-signal questions that separate candidates who've read about ML from those who've shipped and maintained ML at scale.

  1. Google L5 (Staff) — Data Quality: "Your model's prediction distribution shifted from mean 0.3 to 0.7 overnight but accuracy stays the same. What happened?" — This is a label distribution shift: the positive class got more common, but the model's ranking is still correct (AUC unchanged). Root cause: maybe the labeling policy changed (more conservative threshold), or the population changed (holiday effect where more users convert). No action needed if ranking is the goal; if calibration matters (CTR prediction), recalibrate via Platt scaling or isotonic regression on recent data.
  2. Meta E6 (Staff) — Feature Platform: "Your feature pipeline adds a new feature that's highly predictive but has a 6-hour lag. How do you handle training vs serving inconsistency?" — At training time, you have the full feature (6h lag means it's available in offline data). At serving time, the feature is 6h stale. Solution: create two feature views — one with the lagged feature for high accuracy historical training, and one with the latest available version at serving. Or use time-travel: serve the feature value that was available 6h ago, consistent with training. Document the staleness in the feature registry.
  3. Amazon L6 (Senior Principal) — Experimentation: "We launched a new recommendation algorithm and engagement went up 10% per user, but total engagement dropped. Explain." — Network interference: treating users get recommended better content, spend more time, and share less. This means fewer content exposures for control users, reducing their engagement and total content creation. Overall platform health drops even though per-user treatment effect is positive. Solution: cluster-randomized experiment (by social graph partition) or switchback, and measure total-platform metrics (creator-side, total watch time across all users).
  4. Apple ML Staff — Labeling Strategy: "You have $50K to label data for a text classification problem with 100 classes and 500K unlabeled docs. How do you maximize accuracy?" — Step 1: Write 30-50 weak supervision labeling functions (keyword matches, heuristic rules, zero-shot classifier from a small LM). Step 2: Train Snorkel label model to get probabilistic labels for all 500K docs. Step 3: Active learning: use the trained classifier's uncertainty to select 5K examples where the label model is most uncertain. Step 4: Pay humans to label those 5K ($10K). Step 5: Train final classifier on 505K docs (500K noisy + 5K clean). Expected accuracy: 92-95% of fully supervised with 500K labels ($500K).
  5. Microsoft Principal — Incident RCA: "Your search ranking model's NDCG@10 dropped from 0.82 to 0.74 in 30 minutes. Your on-call engineer says 'just rollback.' What do you do?" — Don't rollback blindly. First triage: (1) Check data pipeline — did any feature source fail? If a critical feature (like user history freshness) went missing, predictions degenerate. (2) Check prediction distribution — if all predictions collapsed to a narrow range, a feature pipeline is likely dead. (3) Check for concept drift — did a major event happen (breaking news, product launch) that changed relevance patterns? (4) Check for infrastructure issue — is the model serving pod OOM-ing? (5) Only rollback if none of the above gives a clear answer, but do it fast — every minute at 10K QPS is 600K bad results. Post-mortem: add monitoring for the missing feature case.

✅ Key takeaway: Production ML success depends more on data systems, monitoring, and operational discipline than on model architecture. The best model in the world is worthless if the data pipeline feeding it is broken. Invest in data quality, experiment design, and automated rollback before fancy model architectures.

❌ Common mistakes: Not separating data drift from concept drift (misdiagnosis leads to wrong fix). Naive A/B testing on social platforms without accounting for network interference. Adding features to a production model without data quality checks or staleness documentation. Deploying model updates without shadow/canary stages. Under-investing in monitoring — you can't fix what you can't measure.
